summary: My mother was the only daughter of the Akiyama family, and my father was adopted into her family. She was a very serious person whom I respected deeply. Growing up, I was influenced more by my mother than my father, especially by her teachings about work and responsibility on the farm.

I was born and raised in Nagano Prefecture, which is a rural area about 2,000 feet above sea level. It was very cold there, though it did not snow as much as in Niigata Prefecture. Life in Nagano was peaceful, and our village was of average size with traditional festivals and a Buddhist Zen background.

I remember that Niigata Prefecture had much heavier snowfall compared to Nagano Prefecture where I lived. Though I did not live there, I knew it was colder and snowier, which made a difference in farming and daily life. It was often mentioned when we talked about weather conditions in the region.

The Russo-Japanese War was happening around the time I finished grammar school. My brother went off to serve in the war, which meant I had to take on the role of foreman on our farm since my father was too old to work hard. This war indirectly shaped my early responsibilities and limited my educational opportunities.

I learned about Ninomiya Sontoku at school as part of our moral education. His story was popular in those days and taught us important lessons about life and work. Although my family did not have many books, his teachings were part of the moral science curriculum that I respected.

My family practiced Buddhism, specifically the Zen sect. Most funerals in our country were held in the Buddhist style, and this was the religion I grew up with. However, Buddhist priests did not come to weddings in our farming community, which was different from practices in cities or in America.

Our village followed the Zen sect of Buddhism, which influenced many of our religious practices. The Zen teachings were part of our daily life and community rituals, especially funerals. This sect shaped the spiritual environment in which I was raised in Nagano Prefecture.

In our village, Bon was observed not as a festival but as a time when people stopped working to hold services for the dead. It was an important annual event that brought the community together in remembrance. This tradition was part of the Buddhist customs we followed in Nagano.

We celebrated Tanabata, the Festival of the Weaver Star, by writing poems on narrow strips of paper. This was one of the few festivals I remember from my childhood in the village. It was a simple but meaningful tradition that connected us to the wider cultural practices of Japan.

Although Shinto was present in our country, most funerals were Buddhist rather than Shinto style. Weddings in our farming village were neither Buddhist nor Shinto and were held at home without priests. Shinto priests did officiate ceremonies in cities like Tokyo, but not in rural areas where I lived.

I did not have strong feelings about Tokyo when I was young, but I knew that Buddhist priests might come to weddings there, unlike in my village. Also, when I missed my ship due to a strict eye examination, I waited at Yokohama, near Tokyo. Tokyo represented a big city different from my rural upbringing.

When I was in grade school, I did not think much about big cities like Osaka. It was only later, after reading magazines about foreign countries, that I longed to visit such places. Osaka was one of the large cities I imagined as part of the wider world beyond my village.

I came to America in December 1906 after encouragement from my sister's husband and my brother. America was known as a good place to live and earn money, though I did not know much about it before coming. Upon arrival, I struggled with English but found help among Japanese communities in Seattle and other cities.

I remember hearing vaguely about the Sino-Japanese War and that we won, but I did not have detailed knowledge. Some people I knew were killed in the war, which made it a somber topic. It was part of the background history of my youth in Japan.

I was in America when World War I started. This global conflict was happening while I was working in Seattle and other places, trying to earn money and pay back my passage. The war was a distant event but part of the larger context of my early years abroad.

I left Japan from the port of Yokohama in December 1906 to come to America. When I missed my original ship due to a strict eye exam, I waited at Yokohama and stayed at the Rokkaku Hospital for two weeks. Yokohama was the main gateway for my journey abroad.

After failing the eye examination for trachoma, I stayed at Rokkaku Hospital in Yokohama for two weeks with three other men. The hospital head guaranteed us if we went to America via Canada, which helped me continue my journey. This hospital was a crucial stop before I finally left Japan.

I arrived in Seattle after traveling through Victoria and Vancouver in 1906. I stayed there for about six months, working at sawmills in places like Redmond and Keystone. The Japanese community in Seattle helped me adjust, and I worked hard to pay back my passage money while learning to live in America.

I took a Nippon Yusen Line ship from Yokohama to Seattle as part of my journey to America. This was the main Japanese shipping line at the time, and many young men like me traveled on it. The trip was lively with singing and talking among passengers, though no formal parties were held.

I landed in Victoria, British Columbia, after leaving Japan and stayed there for a week as instructed. The arrival was a relief, and from Victoria, I traveled to Vancouver before entering the United States. Victoria was an important stop on my route to America.

From Victoria, I took a ship to Vancouver and then traveled by train into America. Vancouver was a key transit point for many Japanese immigrants like myself. The Japanese hotels and community there helped newcomers find their way and adjust to the new country.

I worked at sawmills near Redmond, Washington, during my early months in America. The work was tough, involving tasks like putting away sawdust, but many Japanese laborers worked there. Older men from Hawaii helped teach me the job and guided me through the hard work.

Keystone, Washington, was another location where I worked at sawmills after arriving in America. Like Redmond, it had a significant Japanese workforce, and the work was physically demanding. This area was part of my early experience earning money to support myself and repay my passage.

San Francisco was mentioned as one of the cities where Japanese immigrants lived, but I did not have personal experience there during my early years. It was part of the broader Japanese immigrant community in America. My focus was mainly on Seattle and nearby areas at that time.

Los Angeles was another major city with a Japanese population, but like San Francisco, I did not spend time there in my early years. It was known among immigrants but was not part of my personal journey or work experience. My life in America was centered in the Pacific Northwest.

I was part of the Issei generation, the first Japanese immigrants to America. Many Issei like me worked in sawmills and other labor-intensive jobs to earn money and support their families back home. We faced challenges like language barriers and gambling temptations but relied on community support to persevere.
